findings.rationale: Microorganism Information is a specific type of compulsory product information, while Compulsory Additional Information is the broader category encompassing all such data.
findings.qaRationale: Our class captures specific biological content (species/strain of added microorganisms), whereas the GS1 term is a generic structural grouping of compulsory product info; they are different concepts, not a genuine class subsumption.

findings.rationale: Detergent Product is a specific type of product, whereas ProductModel is a generic datasheet/specification for any product.
findings.qaRationale: DetergentProduct denotes an actual detergent good (used as @type alongside gs1:Product), whereas schema:ProductModel denotes a datasheet/prototypical specification of a product, so despite the model-based DPP framing they are different concepts (a thing vs a description of a thing), not a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: DetergentProduct is a class of detergent products, whereas IndividualProduct denotes a single identifiable product instance.
findings.qaRationale: schema:IndividualProduct denotes a serialized single instance, which directly contradicts our explicitly model-based DetergentProduct concept, so they are not the same concept (the apt schema.org target would be Product/ProductModel, not IndividualProduct).

findings.rationale: Detergent Product is a specific type of product, making it a narrower subclass of the general GS1 Product.
findings.qaRationale: DetergentProduct is a regulation-specific subclass of the general GS1 Product (any priceable/orderable item), so ours is strictly narrower.

findings.rationale: Fragrance Allergen is a specific type of allergen detail, making it narrower than the general Allergen Details class.
findings.qaRationale: Our term denotes the allergen substance itself (identified by name and CAS), whereas gs1:AllergenDetails is a descriptor/record structure holding allergen details for a product, so ours is not a narrower kind of that record but a different concept type.

findings.rationale: Fragrance Allergen is a specific type of chemical substance that must be disclosed, whereas ChemicalSubstance is the general class of all chemical substances.
findings.qaRationale: A fragrance allergen identified by name and CAS number is genuinely a kind of chemical substance, so ours is a narrower (more specialized, disclosure-scoped) class of schema.org's general ChemicalSubstance.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the CLP hazard signal word (Danger or Warning) used for chemical products.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties carrying the CLP hazard signal word (Danger/Warning); our detergent term is a local alias of the core concept and is interchangeable in meaning.

findings.rationale: ProductForm is a specific physical form of a detergent, which is a narrower concept than the general GS1 Product.
findings.qaRationale: ProductForm denotes a physical-form attribute concept (e.g. powder, liquid, tablet) of a detergent, not a product entity itself, so it does not match gs1:Product which represents the tradeable item; an attribute class does not match an entity class even though ours could be a value associated with a GS1 Product.

findings.rationale: Product Form is a specific physical form of a detergent, whereas schema.org Product represents any offered product or service; the former is narrower than the latter.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a class denoting the physical form/state of a detergent (an attribute concept), not a product itself, so it is not the same concept as schema.org Product and is not a subtype of it.

findings.rationale: ProductForm is a specific physical form of a detergent, whereas ProductGroup represents a broader grouping of products that vary by attributes.
findings.qaRationale: ProductForm denotes the physical form (e.g. powder, liquid, tablet) of a detergent, an attribute value, whereas schema:ProductGroup is a template grouping product variants, so the two are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a percentage of biodegradability, but the upstream term is generic while ours applies specifically to detergent film.
findings.qaRationale: Our term measures biodegradability specifically of the detergent's water-soluble film, a domain-restricted specialization of the generic upstream biodegradation percentage, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the same concept of a standard test method for biodegradability.
findings.qaRationale: Our class is constrained to ultimate aerobic biodegradability test methods under EU 2026/405 Annex III, a regulation-specific specialization of the generic core BiodegradabilityTestMethod class.

findings.rationale: Biodegradability Test Method is a specific type of evaluation condition, whereas Criterion is a general concept for any assessment.
findings.qaRationale: A standardized test method (a defined measurement procedure) is a different kind of thing from a Criterion (a condition for evaluation/assessment); a method may be referenced by a criterion but is not a subtype of one, so NARROW overstates the link.

findings.rationale: The upstream class represents a general certification concept, while our class specifies a particular biodegradability test method.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a test method (a procedure/standard for measuring biodegradability), whereas schema.org Certification is an authoritative attestation issued by a certification body; these are distinct concepts and not in a genuine broader/narrower hierarchy.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the length of an activity, but our property is specific to biodegradability tests and uses integer days, whereas schema.org’s activityDuration is a general activity duration.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a numeric count of days for a specific biodegradability test bounded to a SurfactantBiodegradability domain, whereas schema.org's activityDuration is a generic Duration for engaging in an activity (different domain, semantics, and datatype), so they are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the measured ultimate aerobic biodegradation expressed as a percentage.
findings.qaRationale: Same underlying measured-biodegradation-percentage concept, but ours is domain-restricted to surfactant aerobic biodegradability with a regulatory pass threshold, making it a specialization of the undefined generic core property rather than fully interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies fragrance allergens above a threshold, whereas the GS1 hasAllergen relation is a general allergen link; thus our term is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Both are allergen-disclosure properties, but GS1 hasAllergen is oriented to general (largely food/consumable) allergen details while ours is the distinct detergent-fragrance-allergen-above-threshold concept, so they overlap without a clean subsumption.

findings.rationale: Our Ingredient class specifies a detergent ingredient with INCI name, functional role and weight‑percentage range, whereas the GS1 class is a broader ingredient detail type for food, beverage and tobacco products.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a chemical detergent ingredient entry (INCI name, functional role, weight-percentage range) while the GS1 class models food/beverage/tobacco ingredient statements, so they are different domain concepts rather than a genus-species pair.

findings.rationale: An Ingredient is a specific type of chemical substance used in a detergent formulation, making it a narrower concept than the general ChemicalSubstance.
findings.qaRationale: Our Ingredient is a formulation entry bundling an INCI-named substance with functional role and weight-percent range, so it overlaps schema:ChemicalSubstance (the matter itself) but is not a clean subtype of it—the entry's compositional/role semantics make it a different concept rather than a narrower species.

findings.rationale: Ingredient is a specific type of chemical substance used in detergent formulations, making it narrower than the general concept of Substance.
findings.qaRationale: Our Ingredient is a formulation line-item (INCI name + functional role + weight-percentage range), i.e. a composition entry, whereas schema:Substance is the chemical matter itself (a MedicalEntity subtype), so it is not a narrower kind of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the intended use of a product, but the candidate is defined for BioChemEntity rather than detergent products.
findings.qaRationale: schema:potentialUse is scoped to a BioChemEntity's use by humans (a pharmacological/biochemical sense), not the general intended application of a product, so despite the surface label overlap it is not the same concept as a detergent product's intendedUse.

findings.rationale: Both describe how a product is to be used, but Intended Use refers to the general purpose while Consumer Usage Instructions provide specific usage instructions.
findings.qaRationale: Intended use describes a product's purpose/application, whereas consumer usage instructions describe directions for how to use it; these answer different questions and are not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the specific method used to perform a measurement, though schema.org’s measurementMethod is generic while our Test Method is specific to biodegradability testing.
findings.qaRationale: Our testMethod is a domain-specific specialization (biodegradability assessment) of schema.org's generic measurementMethod, so ours is narrower than the upstream target rather than merely overlapping.

findings.rationale: Dosage Instructions is a specific type of consumer usage instructions, adding water‑hardness guidance.
findings.qaRationale: Both are free-text product-instruction properties, and dosage instructions (with water-hardness guidance) are a specific subset of the broader consumer usage instructions, so OUR term is narrower.

findings.rationale: The candidate property describes any ingredient’s percentage in the product, while our term specifies phosphorus as a particular ingredient.
findings.qaRationale: Both express a constituent's percentage, but GS1's term is a per-named-ingredient share of total ingredients whereas ours is a weight-percent of aggregate elemental phosphorus, so the differing basis makes it an overlapping rather than a clean narrowing match.

findings.rationale: Both properties express a recommended quantitative amount, though the domain (detergent use vs supplement intake) differs.
findings.qaRationale: schema:recommendedIntake is a nutrition/supplement-intake property (domain DietarySupplement, expressing human consumption guidance) whereas eudet:recommendedDosage is detergent dosing per wash, so despite surface similarity they are distinct domain-specific concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a numeric dose or dosage value, but our term specifies recommended usage per use while the candidate is a generic dose value.
findings.qaRationale: Both express a dose amount, but schema:doseValue is a bare numeric value rooted in medication DoseSchedule whereas ours is a unit-bearing recommended per-use detergent quantity, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Our property requires a gs1:QuantitativeValue (value plus unit), whereas the candidate only provides a numeric value, making our term more specific.
findings.qaRationale: Both express a measured quantity, but ours is a property whose range is the structured gs1:QuantitativeValue (value+unit) container while gs1:value is the bare numeric float component inside such a container, so they overlap in domain without being a clean broader/narrower subsumption.

findings.rationale: Both refer to a link or reference to safety-related documentation, but our term specifically denotes an SDS document whereas the GS1 term is a generic link to any safety information.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking to safety documentation, but our term is restricted to the specific Safety Data Sheet document whereas GS1's safetyInfo is a generic link to any safety information, making ours a narrower specialization.

findings.rationale: Our property represents a list of ingredients in a formulation, whereas the candidate refers to a single ingredient name; thus our term is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Our property points from a detergent product to structured Ingredient objects (a one-to-many composition relation), whereas the GS1 property is a free-text ingredient name string; they are both property-type but model different things, and ours is the narrower, domain-specialized term, so at best a weak NARROW rather than BROAD.

findings.rationale: eudet:ingredientList specifies a structured list of Ingredient entities, whereas GS1’s ingredientStatement is a single string; thus our property is a narrower, more detailed version.
findings.qaRationale: Both convey the product's constituent ingredients, but eudet:ingredientList is a structured list of typed Ingredient entities under INCI nomenclature whereas GS1's ingredientStatement is a single free-text string, making ours a narrower, more specialized expression of the same concept.

findings.rationale: Our property specifies a list of ingredients in the formulation, whereas the candidate refers to general facts about ingredients; thus our term is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern a product's ingredients, but gs1:ingredientsInfo models a link to ingredient facts (a documentary reference) while eudet:ingredientList is a structured INCI-typed enumeration of Ingredient entities, so they overlap topically without a clean genus-species hierarchy.

findings.rationale: eudet:ingredientList refers to a collection of Ingredient entities, whereas schema.org/ingredients denotes a single ingredient used in a recipe.
findings.qaRationale: Both express the constituent ingredients of a product, but schema.org/ingredients is a Recipe-scoped (food) property with a free-text range while eudet:ingredientList targets a detergent formulation with a structured INCI Ingredient range, so they overlap conceptually without being a broad/narrow taxonomic pair; the first-pass BROAD rests on plurality, which is the wrong axis.

findings.rationale: The candidate property covers any recipe ingredient or list, while our term specifically lists detergent ingredients; thus ours is a narrower specialization.
findings.qaRationale: schema:recipeIngredient is semantically bound to culinary Recipe entities (food/cooking quantities like '1 cup of sugar'), whereas our term denotes INCI-coded chemical components of a detergent formulation — a superficial 'ingredient' name overlap, not the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the set of hazardous substances present in a product, with identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both express the same hazardous-substances relation reusing oec:HazardousSubstance as range, but our property is domain-restricted to DetergentProduct whereas the core property is unrestricted, making ours a narrower specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both classes represent the CLP signal words used to indicate hazard severity in chemical products.
findings.qaRationale: Both are signal-word classes for chemical hazard severity, but ours is explicitly scoped to EU CLP Art. 20 while the undefined upstream HazardSignalWord likely covers GHS signal words generally, so they overlap rather than being provably interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent CLP precautionary statements for a detergent product, with identical domain and range semantics.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote CLP precautionary statements with identical xsd:string range, but our term is domain-restricted to eudet:DetergentProduct whereas the core property is product-agnostic, making ours the narrower specialization rather than interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent GHS hazard pictogram codes (e.g., GHS01–GHS09) for a product, matching in definition and intended use.
findings.qaRationale: OUR property's range is xsd:string carrying multiple GHS pictogram codes (GHS01-GHS09) per CLP, whereas the singular label 'hazardPictogramCode' implies a single code and the undefined upstream term gives no basis to confirm matching meaning, so I reject rather than assert equivalence.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent CLP hazard statements (e.g., H302, H315) for a detergent product.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties for chemical hazard statements, but the upstream term lacks any definition to confirm it is CLP-specific and same granularity (singular 'hazardStatement' vs our plural string list), so interchangeability cannot be guaranteed.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the physical form or shape of a detergent product, matching in scope and intent.
findings.qaRationale: Both denote the physical form of a product, but GS1's is a free-text form *description* general to all products while ours is a detergent-scoped property pointing to a coded ProductForm enumeration, so they overlap strongly without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe the physical form of a product, though one is for detergents and the other for drugs.
findings.qaRationale: schema:dosageForm is a pharmaceutical-specific concept denoting the dose form of a drug/supplement, not the generic physical form of a detergent, so despite the superficial 'form' similarity they are different concepts.
